kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|59.10|N|132|44|2.67|E|}}}} }} [[File:Mitsujo Kofun, enkei.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|No.2 on left,Mitsujō Kofun on right}}]] The {{nihongo|'''Mitsujō Kofun'''|三ツ城古墳| Mitsujō kofun}} is a group of three [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]] in the [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of [[:en:Aki Province|Kingdom of Aki]]. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from [[:en:Higashi-Hiroshima Station|Higashi-Hiroshima Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]]

kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|59.10|N|132|44|2.67|E|}}}} }} [[File:Mitsujo Kofun, enkei.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|No.2 on left,Mitsujō Kofun on right}}]] The {{nihongo|'''Mitsujō Kofun'''|三ツ城古墳| Mitsujō kofun}} is a group of three [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]] in the [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of [[:en:Aki Province|Kingdom of Aki]]. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from [[:en:Higashi-Hiroshima Station|Higashi-Hiroshima Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]]

[[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]] in the [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of [[:en:Aki Province|Kingdom of Aki]]. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from [[:en:Higashi-Hiroshima Station|Higashi-Hiroshima Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]]

It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of [[:en:Aki Province|Kingdom of Aki]]. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from [[:en:Higashi-Hiroshima Station|Higashi-Hiroshima Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]]

region|San'yō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of [[:en:Aki Province|Kingdom of Aki]]. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from [[:en:Higashi-Hiroshima Station|Higashi-Hiroshima Station]] on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].

A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Mitsujō Kofun | native_name = 三ツ城古墳 | region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} | type = ''[[kofun]]'' cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 4th century AD | abandoned Japan]]}}{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|59.10|N|132|44|2.67|E|}}}} }} [[File:Mitsujo Kofun, enkei.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|No.2 on left,Mitsujō Kofun on right}}]] The {{nihongo|'''Mitsujō Kofun'''|三ツ城古墳| Mitsujō kofun}} is a group of three [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}}

There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}}

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Mitsujō Kofun | native_name = 三ツ城古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = 090430 Mitsujo Kofun Cluster aerial.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Mitsujō Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = 10 | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.41633333|N|132.73388889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} | type = ''[[kofun]]'' cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 4th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}}{{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|59.10|N|132|44|2.67|E|}}}} }} [[File:Mitsujo Kofun, enkei.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|No.2 on left,Mitsujō Kofun on right}}]] The {{nihongo|'''Mitsujō Kofun'''|三ツ城古墳| Mitsujō kofun}} is a group of three [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}}

There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki|1=Aki Province}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station|1=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].

The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki|1=Aki Province}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station|1=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].

On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki|1=Aki Province}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station|1=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}}

}} [[File:Mitsujo Kofun, enkei.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|No.2 on left,Mitsujō Kofun on right}}]] The {{nihongo|'''Mitsujō Kofun'''|三ツ城古墳| Mitsujō kofun}} is a group of three [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region|1=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki|1=Aki Province}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|WD=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station|1=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].

==Overview== The Mitsujō Kofun is a {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and is the largest keyhole-shaped Kofun in Hiroshima Prefecture. It is located at the edge of a hill on the southern edge of the Saijō Basin. With a total length of about 92 meters, it was constructed in three tiers, with an anterior width of about 67 meters and a posterior circular portion with a diameter of about 62 meters and a height of about 13 meters. It is orientated to the east-northeast. On the outer surface, in addition to ''[[fukiishi]]'', a row of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' (including "morning glory-shaped" ''haniwa'') and figurative ''haniwa'' (house-shaped, lid-shaped, chicken-shaped, waterfowl-shaped, shield-shaped, dove-shaped, helmet-shaped, short armor-shaped, etc.) have been found. There were about 1,800 ''haniwa'' in the entire mound. A dry moat with a depth of about one meter surrounded the tumulus.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Three box-shaped [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s have been confirmed in the posterior tumulus, two of which contained stone sarcophagi. Metalware such as {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}s, iron swords and knives, bronze scepters, and beads have been unearthed, as well as {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|qid=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Based on the construction techniques and recovered artifacts, it was believed that the Mitsujō Kofun was built in the middle of the Kofun period (5th century), and was the burial location of the chiefs of the southern part of {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|qid=Q965029|lt=Kingdom of Aki|1=Aki Province}}. In 2009, it was announced that the construction date of the Mitsujō Kofun had been determined to have been 412 AD, based on research into the excavated Sue pottery. It is the first time that the date of a kofun had been determined with such precision.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
To the south of the Mitsujō Kofun is a round {{nihongo|''{{ill|Round Kofun|lt=empun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style tumulus (designated "Mitsujō Kofun No. 2") with a diameter of about 25 meters and height of four meters. It predates the Mitsujō Kofun. A third tumulus, designed "Mitsujō Kofun No.3" is a four by eight meter oval mound, and intersects with the moat of Kofun No.2. It was constructed in the late Kofun period and thus postdates both the Mitsujō Kofun and Mitsujō Kofun No. 2.
The site has been restored and is maintained as a historical park. The site is about 15 minutes by car from {{ill|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|en|Higashi-Hiroshima Station|qid=Q1136538|lt=Higashi-Hiroshima Station|1=Higashi-Hiroshima Station}} on the [[:en:Sanyo Shinkansen|Sanyo Shinkansen]].
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|qid=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

}} [[File:Mitsujo Kofun, enkei.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|No.2 on left,Mitsujō Kofun on right}}]] The {{nihongo|'''Mitsujō Kofun'''|三ツ城古墳| Mitsujō kofun}} is a group of three [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Nakajō-chūō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Higashihiroshima, Hiroshima|Higashihiroshima]], {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}} in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|qid=Q398087|lt=San'yō region|1=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The cluster was collectively designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1982 with the area under protection expanded in 2001.<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/217399|title=三ツ城古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1954 and from 1987 to 1991.
